title: Baked Apples with Sultanas
subtitle: Bramley apples cored, filled with sultanas and brown sugar, and baked until soft
excerpt: Large Bramley apples cored and filled with a mixture of sultanas, butter, and brown sugar, then baked in a low oven until the flesh is completely soft and the skin splits and caramelises. The filling melts into a syrup that pools at the base of each apple. Served with cream or custard.
sourceNotes: Baked apples are one of the oldest British dessert preparations, appearing in cookery writing from at least the seventeenth century. The filling of dried fruit, butter, and sugar is the standard British version; variants exist with marzipan, spiced fruit, or nut fillings.

content.text: Bramley apples are the best for baking whole. The flesh softens fast and the skin keeps the apple upright in the dish, instead of spreading into a puddle. The score around the middle is not for show. It stops the skin splitting unevenly and lets the apple expand without cracking open on one side.

The butter and sugar in the filling melt and combine with the apple juices to form a syrup at the base of the roasting tin. This is worth spooning over the apples partway through baking and again when serving.

Heat the oven to 180°C fan. Core {{apple-bramley}} of Bramley apples, making a tunnel about 3 cm wide through the centre. Score a line around the equator of each apple through the skin only, do not cut into the flesh. Place in a roasting tin.

Mix {{sultanas}} of sultanas, {{soft-brown-sugar}} of soft brown sugar, {{unsalted-butter}} of softened butter, and {{cinnamon-ground}} of ground cinnamon in a small bowl. Stir to a rough paste. Pack the filling into the cored centre of each apple. Mound it slightly above the top.

Add 4 tablespoons of water to the base of the roasting tin. Bake for 40, 45 minutes until the flesh is completely soft when a skewer is pushed through with no resistance, and the skin has split along the scored line. Spoon the pan juices over each apple halfway through baking. Rest for 5 minutes and serve with cold cream or warm custard, spooning the pan juices over at the table.

items.fix: Score through the skin only. Check at 40 minutes, Bramley apples can go from soft to collapsed quite quickly.
items.cause: The scored line was cut too deeply, or the baking time was too long
items.symptom: Apple split and collapsed rather than holding its shape

items.fix: Continue baking in 5-minute increments until a skewer passes through the flesh with no resistance. Very large Bramleys can take up to an hour.
items.cause: Oven temperature too low, or apples were unusually large
items.symptom: Apple is still firm in the centre after 45 minutes

items.fix: Cover the exposed filling top loosely with a small piece of foil. Lower the oven to 170°C fan and extend the baking time.
items.cause: Filling was too exposed, or oven was too hot
items.symptom: Filling burned on top before the apple cooked through

items.fix: Add 4 tablespoons of water at the start. If the tin is large, crowd the apples together or use a smaller tin to keep them close and retain moisture.
items.cause: Not enough water was added, or the tin was too large and the liquid evaporated
items.symptom: No syrup in the base of the tin

content.text: Baked apples are one of the oldest British puddings, written down from at least the 1600s. The filling of dried fruit, sugar, and spice reflects the way British cooks used dried fruit for a long time: as a sweetener, a flavouring, and a way to stretch a dish. The recipe has stayed much the same for four hundred years because the method works whatever filling you use.
